coredata.dc:title: Preparation of chitosan/gelatin-based functional films integrated with carbon dots from banana peel for active packaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Sul, Yoonjung
Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were manufactured with banana peels using a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 6 h). The synthesized CDs were spherical particles with a size of 1–3 nm having carboxyl groups and amine groups on the surface. CDs have been impregnated into chitosan/gelatin films to synthesize multifunctional packaging films. The composite film showed a slight decrease in transparency but a significant increase in UV protection properties. The fabricated film displayed strong antioxidant efficacy showing >74 % DPPH and 99 % ABTS radical scavenging potential. The film also unveiled substantial antibacterial activity against the foodborne pathogenic bacteria, Listeria monocytogenes, fully eliminating the growth of these bacteria within 6 h of exposure. The chitosan/gelatin film containing CD was used for minced meat packaging, and the film delayed bacterial growth (< 1 Log CFU/g after 24 h) and maintained the meat color even after 24 h of storage at 20 °C. The CD-added chitosan/gelatin functional film has a high probability of application in active food packaging, especially for extending the shelf life of packaged meat and maintaining its aesthetic quality.

head.ce:title: Preparation of chitosan/gelatin-based functional films integrated with carbon dots from banana peel for active packaging application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were manufactured with banana peels using a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 6 h). The synthesized CDs were spherical particles with a size of 1–3 nm having carboxyl groups and amine groups on the surface. CDs have been impregnated into chitosan/gelatin films to synthesize multifunctional packaging films. The composite film showed a slight decrease in transparency but a significant increase in UV protection properties. The fabricated film displayed strong antioxidant efficacy showing >74 % DPPH and 99 % ABTS radical scavenging potential. The film also unveiled substantial antibacterial activity against the foodborne pathogenic bacteria, Listeria monocytogenes, fully eliminating the growth of these bacteria within 6 h of exposure. The chitosan/gelatin film containing CD was used for minced meat packaging, and the film delayed bacterial growth (< 1 Log CFU/g after 24 h) and maintained the meat color even after 24 h of storage at 20 °C. The CD-added chitosan/gelatin functional film has a high probability of application in active food packaging, especially for extending the shelf life of packaged meat and maintaining its aesthetic quality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring food safety and maintaining good quality throughout the food supply chain is a major challenge for the food industry. The food industry uses various packaging strategies such as active packaging, modified gas packaging, and intelligent packaging systems to provide high-quality food and extend the shelf life of packaged products [1,2]. Among various procedures, active food packaging is used to maintain the quality of fresh food by protecting food from microbial contamination and preventing deterioration by biochemical changes in food [3,4]. Various functional nanomaterials have been employed to develop active packaging films [5].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based spherical nanomaterial with a size of <10 nm, have been in the spotlight due to their water solubility, cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high functionalities such as strong antioxidant, antibacterial, and light protection properties [6]. Due to their small size, these CDs exhibit unique photoluminescent properties that distinguish them from other carbon-based nanomaterials [7,8]. Besides photoluminescence, the surface functional groups of CDs display other useful functional properties such as UV protection and antioxidant and antibacterial properties. These properties make it a promising candidate for exploring new applications in active packaging films [9]. For example, Ezati and Rhim have created composite films based on pectin and CDs for light conversion. They claimed that adding CDs improved the film's UV protection ability by converting UV photons into blue light [10]. In addition, increasing CD concentration enhanced composite films' antibacterial and antioxidant properties.

CDs are manufactured using various synthetic methods classified as top-down and bottom-up methods [11,12], for which they can be prepared using natural resources that determine the complex chemical form of CDs. Attempts have been made to construct CDs from cheap, renewable supplies of primary materials. In this regard, a plan to utilize agricultural and fishery food processing waste or by-products as a sustainable resource for CD manufacturing is gathering expectations [10,11]. One of the biowaste resources that can be used for CD manufacturing is banana peel, and the high value-added use of this huge by-product is encouraging in terms of reducing agricultural waste and efficiently using underused natural resources [12]. The nutritional value of banana peels varies depending on the variety and stage of maturity. Banana peels have been shown to contain many nutrients and minerals. Banana peels contain carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and fiber as the main source of many bioactive compounds with various functions. The high carbohydrate content of banana peels ranges from 59 to 76 %, making them a good material for sustainable CD synthesis [13,14]. These CDs can be used as functional nanofillers to improve packaging films' physical properties or provide functional properties [15].

Various polymers were used to anchor and support functional fillers in manufacturing active packaging films [16]. For many years, scientists and the packaging industry have focused on using biopolymers as alternatives to plastic packaging to reduce the environmental burden caused by plastic packaging waste [17]. Chitosan, one of these biopolymers, is widely used in food packaging due to its low cost, biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, viscosity, and excellent film-forming ability. It is a linear polysaccharide composed of (1–4)-2-acetamido-2-deoxy-β-D-glucan and (1–4)-2-amino-2-deoxy-β-D-glucan produced from chitin, found primarily in the exoskeletons of crustaceans and insects or in the cell walls of molds and yeasts [18]. When used as a packaging polymer, chitosan's reactive amino and hydroxyl groups can create hydrogen bonds to increase strength [19]. However, the use of chitosan film is limited due to its low mechanical strength and gas barrier properties. The properties of the chitosan film can be improved by blending it with a biopolymer such as gelatin [20].

Gelatin is a polypeptide produced by partial hydrolysis of collagen and is known to form a transparent, strong, oxygen gas barrier film with non-toxic, biodegradable, and excellent film-forming properties [21]. When exposed to radiation, gelatin is susceptible to deterioration because tyrosine and phenylalanine residues generate free radicals, and major by-products such as prolyl-hydroxyproline and glycine are formed when gelatin breaks down [22]. With significant improvements in UV protection properties, water vapor permeability, and mechanical properties, researchers evaluated various physicochemical properties of gelatin- and chitosan-based films and recommended them as suitable candidates for food packaging applications [23]. In addition, chitosan/gelatin-based films had significant antioxidant and antibacterial activity [20,24]. Although there are some reports on the fabrication of active packaging films based on CDs, there are no studies on the fabrication and application of chitosan/gelatin-based films using CDs prepared from banana peels for active packaging applications.

Therefore, the main objective of this study was to prepare a multifunctional active packaging material by adding CD derived from banana peel to a mixture of chitosan and gelatin. To this end, CD was synthesized using a banana peel, its characteristics were analyzed, and functional chitosan/gelatin-based film with CD was manufactured to investigate the properties of the film. In addition, the possibility of extending the shelf life of packaged beef by using a functional film for meat packaging was investigated.

Food-grade gelatin (200 Bloom, Type A) obtained from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, Korea). Chitosan (Ch) (degree of deacetylation: 75–85 %, MW: 190,000–310,000 based on viscosity, viscosity: 200–800 cP at 1 % acetic acid,), 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Foodborne pathogenic bacteria such as L. monocytogenes ATCC 15313 and E. coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895 were supplied by the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC, Seoul, South Korea). The banana (Musa acuminate L.) for CDs synthesis and minced beef for the packaging application was brought from a local supermarket (Seoul, South Korea).

Fresh banana peels were used to fabricate CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method. For this, 1 g of banana peel powder was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water by stirring, then transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel reactor lined with Teflon and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted material. The supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (Germany; diameter: 25 mm; pore size: 0.22 μm) to remove impurities. The filtrate was kept in a refrigerator (4 °C) until further testing.

The microstructure of the CD was observed through a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) to confirm the shape and size of the synthesized CD. Average particle size and size distribution were evaluated using ImageJ software. Light absorption and fluorescence responses were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax, Horiba Inc., MA, USA), respectively. Functional groups and elemental composition of the synthesized CDs were evaluated using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, IFS 66 V/S, Bruker) in the range of 4000–500 cm−1, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a K-alpha X-Ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Massachusetts, USA).

The antioxidant activity of CD at various concentrations (25, 50, and 100 g/mL) was evaluated using DPPH and ABTS methods [19]. Ten milliliters of DPPH and ABTS test solutions were mixed with a predefined volume of CD solution, and the mixture was left at room temperature for 30 min. The absorbance was then recorded at 537 nm and 734 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series spectrophotometer, Seoul, Korea) and determined the antioxidant activity as follows:
Antioxidant activity

Ao and AT are the absorbance values of the control and CD samples, respectively.

The antimicrobial activity of CDs was evaluated using the agar well diffusion and disk diffusion methods against foodborne pathogenic bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli). An inoculum of 6 Log CFU/mL of the test bacteria was spread on the surface of agar plates using a sterilized swab. Afterward, wells with a diameter of 8 mm were punched into the agar, filled with 100 μL of CD (4 mg/mL), and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, then the diameter of the inhibition zone was measured.

Chitosan/gelatin-based films were made through the solution-casting method [25]. For this, 2 g each of gelatin and chitosan and 1.2 g of glycerol (30 wt% based on polymers) were mixed in 150 mL of distilled water containing 1 % acetic acid with strong mechanical agitation at 80 °C for 30 min. After that, 0.12 g of CD (3 wt% based on polymers) was gradually added to the solution while it was vigorously stirred for an hour. The concentration of CD (3 %) was chosen based on the functional activity of the resulting nanoparticles through preliminary testing. The film-forming solution was immediately cast onto a glass plate covered with Teflon film (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried for two days at 25 °C. The dried film was peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % RH for at least two days before further testing. For comparison, neat chitosan/gelatin films were prepared using the same procedure without adding CD. The prepared films were designated chitosan/gelatin and chitosan/gelatin/CD films.

The film samples' surface and cross-sectional morphology were investigated using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., S4 Matsuda, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 8 kV.

The types of functional groups were determined by recording Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) spectra using an FTIR spectrometer (IFS 66 V/S, Bruker Optics, Gwangmyeong-si, Gyeonggi-do, Korea) in the range of 4000 to 500 cm−1.

The surface color of the films was determined using a Chroma meter (Konica Minolta CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan) according to the method of Ezati and Rhim [26]. The UV barrier and transparency of the film were evaluated by measuring the light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660), respectively, using a spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea).
The film thickness was measured using a digital micrometer (Digimatic Micrometer, QuantuMike IP 65, Mitutoyo, Japan) with a precision of 1 μm [27].

Mechanical properties of the film, such as tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and elastic modulus (EM), were determined using Instron universal testing machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA) according to the standard method of ASTM D 882-88. For this, a rectangular film strip (10 cm × 2.5 cm) was attached to the machine and performed a tensile test with an initial grip separation of 50 mm and a cross-head speed of 10 mm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The gravimetric cup method was used to determine the WVP of the films following the ASTM E96–95 standard method described by Ezati and Rhim [28].

The surface wettability of the tested samples was determined using the contact angle analyzer (Phoneix 150, Surface Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., Kunpo, Korea), following the method described by Ezati and Rhim [28].
The antioxidant activity of the films was determined using the DPPH and ABTS methods, as described by Min et al. [29].
The antibacterial activity of the films was tested using the total viable colony count method against L. monocytogenes and E. coli following the method of Kousheh et al. [30].

The packaging effect of chitosan/gelatin/CD film was investigated through a minced beef packaging test. For this, 50 g of minced beef was wrapped using a vacuum sealer in PE film (control), and the neat chitosan/gelatin and chitosan/gelatin/CD films as functional films (10 cm × 11 cm) to compare the functionality of the chitosan/gelatin-based films. The packaged meat samples were stored at 20 °C for 48 h, and their visual changes, surface color, and the growth of bacteria were evaluated using the total viable count method.

The total viable colony count method was used to evaluate the number of bacteria in packaged minced beef samples. Meat samples (5 g minced beef in 45 mL of 0.1 % peptone water) were homogenized in a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France) for 180 s to evaluate the bactericidal activity. Then, 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates were replicated with 1 mL of serially diluted homogeneous aliquots. Colonies were enumerated after 48 h of incubation at 37 °C, and the experimental results were presented as Log CFU/g [31].

Film properties were measured with individually prepared films in triplicate as replicated experimental units, and the results were provided with a mean ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significant differences between the film properties were determined (p < 0.05) with Duncan's multiple range tests using the SPSS statistical analysis program (SPSS 21, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The morphology of the CDs was observed using TEM, as shown in Fig. 1a. TEM results validate that the CDs are spherical with a size distribution in the proton range (<10 nm) and are monodisperse with particle diameters ranging from 1.55 to 3.55 nm [32].

The optical features of the CDs were investigated using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. Due to quantum confinement, aqueous CD solutions emit a bright blue color when exposed to UV radiation at 360 nm (Fig. 1b,c). The instantaneous color change under UV irradiation is a distinctive phenomenon of CDs. The carbon source and manufacturing process used to create CDs greatly affect the intensity of color change in CDs [33]. The UV–Vis light absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 1d., which shows a similar light absorption pattern and a comparable absorption peak compatible with previously reported CDs created by Ezati et al. [33]. It displays a characteristic absorption peak at 298 nm due to the n-π* transitions (CO or C-OH bonds) and a shouldering peak at 282 nm attributed to the π-π* transitions (CC bonds with sp2 hybridization) [33].

The fluorescence properties of CDs were also studied using fluorescence spectroscopy. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of the CD is shown in Fig. 1e. The spectrum exhibited a normal excitation wavelength-dependent response, and the emission wavelength was red-shifted during excitation. [34]. As a result, the emission peak's clear and consistent bathochromic shift was observed when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 440 nm. In general, it has been suggested that the tunable emission of CDs results from different fluorescent properties of different sizes or the presence of different emission sites on the surface of CDs [35–37]. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity was observed at about 440 nm when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm. In addition, many fluorescence features, such as size distribution and different luminescent sites on the CD surface, contribute to the tunable luminescence quality [38]. However, additional investigations are needed to determine the exact fluorescence process responsible for the excitation wavelength-dependent emission.

XPS analysis was used to investigate the chemical composition and presence of constituents of the CDs. Fig. 2a shows the complete survey spectrum of CDs. The 284.52, 398.97, and 530.69 eV peaks correspond to the surface functional groups N and O and carbon (C 1s of sp2 C), respectively [39].

It also showed that the surface of the CDs contained 68.56 % of C 1s, 10.18 % of O 1s, and 21.16 % of N 1s at the relevant binding energies. The deconvoluted XPS C1s spectrum of CD (Fig. 2b) can be divided into four fitted peaks, the CH or CC bonds at peak 282.53 eV, the CN and CO bonds at peaks 284.99 and 285.78 eV, and the O-C-O or CO of the carbonyl group at peak 287.18 eV [39]. Fig. 2c shows that the high-resolution XPS O 1s spectra fragmented into three peaks with energies of 530.2 eV, 531.8 eV, and 532.4 eV, respectively, assigned to the C-O/N-O, CO, and C-O-H groups (Fig. 2d) [39,40]. Additionally, the deconvoluted XPS N1s spectra fit three component peaks at energies of 398.5, 399.2, and 400.0 eV, respectively, which were attributed to tertiary N bonds to carbon atoms in the form of N-(C)3 and NH bonds [39,41].

Antioxidant activity is the most common indicator to determine the ability of substances to scavenge or neutralize free radicals. The antioxidant activity of CD was evaluated through ABTS and DPPH methods, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The CD showed significant antioxidant activity in both methods, and its ability to scavenge ABTS and DPPH free radicals increased significantly with increasing concentrations of CD. At a concentration of 25 μg/mL, CD exhibited about 36.1 % and 17.5 % of free radical scavenging ability for ABTS and DPPH free radicals, respectively, and antioxidant activity gradually increased with concentration. At 100 μg/mL, CD, The antioxidant activity reached 98 % and 93 %, respectively. The antioxidant activity of CD measured by the ABTS method was higher than that measured by the DPPH method, which was attributed to the hydrophilic nature of CD. The hydrophilic CD interacted more readily with the ABTS aqueous solution than the methanolic DPPH solution. The antioxidant activity of CD is due to surface oxygenation functional groups, which can scavenge and quench ABTS and DPPH radicals by transferring H• from -OH to ABTS-H and DPPH-H on CD [42]. The radicals of ABTS and DPPH can be quenched through the combined effects of hydrogen donation, electron transfer, and radical adduct generation provided by CDs. Several studies have reported that CD can exert antioxidant activity on biopolymer matrices. The radical neutralizing ability of CD and its substantial potential as an active filler in food packaging technology has been previously reported [43].

The antibacterial efficacy of CD against foodborne bacterial strains (Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli) was evaluated using the agar-well diffusion approach and the disk diffusion method, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The CD showed inhibition zones of 0 mm and 5 mm for E. coli and L. monocytogenes, respectively, in the agar-well diffusion method and 0 mm and 2 mm, in the disk diffusion method. CD exhibited distinct antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes in agar-well diffusion and disk diffusion methods. The antibacterial activity of CD is well known and agrees well with the results of this study [44]. The current results show that Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes) are more sensitive to CD than Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli), mainly due to differences in cell wall composition and CD binding affinity of these bacteria [45]. Because they lack an outer membrane, Gram-positive bacteria are more susceptible to the damaging effects of ROS, which can penetrate and degrade the cell wall [45]. The exact mechanism for the antibacterial action of CDs has not yet been elucidated, but several research groups suggest that the small size of CDs can quickly penetrate bacterial cells and alter cellular metabolism, which is thought to be responsible for their antibacterial effects [46–48].

Generally, when a composite film is prepared by adding functional components to a biopolymer, its appearance and optical properties may change depending on their interaction. The apparent images of the chitosan/gelatin-based films are shown in Fig. 5a. The neat chitosan/gelatin film had a pale-yellow appearance, but the CD-added film (chitosan/gelatin/CD film) had a dark yellow tint, which was attributed to the brown-yellow color of the CD. Table 1 shows the surface color properties of the chitosan/gelatin-based films. The addition of the CD significantly (p < 0.05) decreased the brightness (L-value) of the chitosan/gelatin film while significantly increasing both the yellowness (b-value) and redness (a-value) of the film due to the brownish-yellow tint of the CD. As a result, the film's total color difference (ΔE) increased significantly with increasing a and b-values.

One of the key functions of food packaging film is transparency and UV protection. The light transmittance of the film was measured to evaluate the transparency and UV-blocking function of the film, as shown in Fig. 5b. The chitosan/gelatin film exhibited high light transmittance in the visible light of 400 nm or more and high film transparency but showed low light transmittance in the ultraviolet light below 400 nm, especially UV-B light (280–320 nm), resulting in some UV blocking properties., which is attributable to chitosan and gelatin's UV-blocking ability [30]. However, the addition of CD significantly reduced the transmittance of visible light and almost completely blocked the transmittance of UV-B rays. Table 1 shows the results of evaluating the film's transparency and UV-barrier qualities using light transmittance measurements at 660 nm (T660) and 280 nm (T280). Adding CD reduced the film's T660 slightly (by 7.5 %) while dramatically reducing its T280 (down to 0.07 %), indicating that adding CD gives the film full UV protection at the slightest sacrifice in its transparency. The UV-barrier property of the CD-added chitosan/gelatin composite film is due to the CD's light scattering and absorption capacity [30]. A similar UV-blocking effect was observed in a bacterial cellulose film containing CD produced using lactic acid bacteria [30]. Chitosan/gelatin/CD films with high UV-blocking properties can protect packaged products from photo-oxidation and photo-degradation reactions caused by UV light.

The microstructure of the chitosan/gelatin-based film was investigated by FE-SEM, as shown in Fig. 5c. Surface SEM images of the chitosan/gelatin film did not reveal any voids or cracks in the film. The surface structure of the film was not affected by the addition of 3 wt% CD. The microstructure of the composite film was more compact and smooth than the neat chitosan/gelatin film and was free of voids and cracks. The integral appearance without holes, ruptures, voids, or cavities can also be seen in the FE-SEM images of the cross-section, indicating good compatibility of CDs with the chitosan/gelatin matrix and uniform distribution of CDs in the chitosan/gelatin matrix.

The FTIR spectra of the chitosan/gelatin-based films are illustrated in Fig. 5d. The FTIR spectra of the chitosan/gelatin-based films show a wide band at 3292 cm−1 due to the OH stretching and overlaps in the same space with the vibrational stretching of NH groups. Stretching vibrations of the -C-H or -CH2 groups were evidenced in the band between 2943 and 2871 cm−1 [49]. The peaks at 1634, 1548, and 1238 cm−1 were due to CO stretching of amide-I, NH bending of amide-II, and CN & NH stretching of amide-III in the chitosan/gelatin-based films [50,51]. Asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of -COO were confirmed in the FTIR band of 1405 cm−1 [52]. There was evidence of stretching vibrations of CO in the FTIR bands around 1198 and 1040 cm−1 [53]. The CD-added chitosan/gelatin film showed FTIR patterns similar to the neat chitosan/gelatin film except for a slight difference in peak intensity. Physical interactions (H-bonds and van der Waals) between the phenolic groups of the CDs and the polymer matrix may be responsible for the slight variation in peak intensity [54]. The FTIR analysis results demonstrated that adding CD to the polymer matrix did not affect the chemical composition of the polymer chains. Instead, the CD was dispersed throughout the matrix, resulting in the physical interaction with the polymer matrix.

The thermal stability of the chitosan/gelatin-based films was investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and the results of TGA thermograms are shown in Fig. 5e. Both the neat and CD-added chitosan/gelatin films showed three distinct thermal degradation patterns. The initial thermal decomposition in the first step (50–120 °C) can be attributed to the evaporation of water physically adsorbed on the film, followed by the breaking of intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonds [55]. A second thermal degradation occurred between 120 and 285 °C, which was attributed to the thermal decomposition of glycerol added as a plasticizer [56]. The major mass loss of the films occurred between 280 and 420 °C, which was attributed to the decomposition of the high molecular weight polymer backbone and CDs [57]. As shown in the TGA thermogram, it can be seen that CD affected the thermal stability of the chitosan/gelatin film. Hydrophilic CDs containing physically adsorbed water molecules cause CD-added films to deteriorate more slowly in the early stages of the process. That is, the maximum temperature of the CD-added film was slightly higher than that of the neat chitosan/gelatin film. Compared to the neat chitosan/gelatin film, the CD-added film degraded less in stages 2 and 3 and was more thermally stable. The TGA data also show that the final residual char contents at 600 °C of chitosan/gelatin and chitosan/gelatin/CD films are relatively high, 30 % and 38 %, respectively, mainly due to the non-ignitable mineral and impurities in the polymers [65]. The enhanced thermal stability of the CD-added chitosan/gelatin film may be due to the increased interaction through hydrogen bonding between the CD and the hydroxyl groups of the polymer backbone. Wang et al. reported that adding CD enhanced the chitosan-based film's thermal stability [52].

Food products must be protected during storage and transportation by packaging films with adequate mechanical strength and integrity to avoid breakage or leaking. Table 2 displays the mechanical characteristics of the gelatin/chitosan-based films. There was no significant difference (p < 0.05) in the film thickness between the chitosan/gelatin-based films, indicating that the small-sized CDs were uniformly distributed in the polymer matrix, as seen in the FE-SEM images (Fig. 5c). The neat chitosan/gelatin films are relatively strong and stiff with 52.1 MPa for TS and 2.7 GPa for EM, but less flexible with 6.1 % for EB. When CD was added, the TS of the chitosan/gelatin film increased significantly (p < 0.05), but the EB decreased significantly (p < 0.05) without changing the EM of the film. The CD added to the film interacted with the polymer to enhance the film strength and restrict the polymer strand's molecular movement to reduce flexibility [18], which was attributed to the more compact structure of the film due to the uniformly dispersed CD in the polymer matrix and hydrogen bonding between surface functional groups. A similar effect of CD addition on enhancing mechanical strength has been frequently observed in various biopolymer-based films such as gelatin/chitosan [18], gelatin [23], bacterial nanocellulose [24], gelatin/carrageenan [25], pectin [49] films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
ce:section.ce:para: The WCA and WVP of the chitosan/gelatin-based films are also shown in Table 2. Water contact angle (WCA) is used as an index to evaluate a film's surface wettability or hydrophilicity. The WCA of the neat chitosan/gelatin film was 73.6°, indicating that the film has a slightly hydrophobic surface. The addition of CD significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the WCA of the film due to the hydrophilic nature of CD. However, the film-maintained surface hydrophobicity with a WCA greater than 65°.

The WVP of composite films is strongly influenced by the type of filler and its interaction with the polymer matrix. Water vapor transport through a film is mediated by several physical processes, including absorption of water vapor on the film's surface, diffusion of water molecules, and vaporization of water on the other side of the film. The WVP of the neat chitosan/gelatin film was 1.24 × 10−9 g m/m2 Pa s, and the addition of the CD did not significantly change it. The WVP of the chitosan/gelatin film does not appear to be significantly affected by adding a small amount of CD (3 wt% based on polymer). The WVP of agar-based films was previously shown to be unaffected by the addition of low levels (2 wt%) of CD but increased significantly with increasing CD concentration [43]. During storage and distribution, food quality deteriorates due to moisture movement between food and the environment. Therefore, packaging films with low WVP values are preferred to preserve food quality and extend the shelf life of packaged foods.

Antibacterial properties are essential for packaging materials used in active food packaging to ensure food safety and extend shelf life [58]. The antibacterial activity of the chitosan/gelatin-based films was quantitatively evaluated using a total colony count method against L. monocytogenes and E. coli, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . The strains used for antibacterial testing in this study are common foodborne pathogenic bacteria commonly used in food packaging research, so these bacteria were used to demonstrate the antibacterial activity of the developed CDs and films. The neat chitosan/gelatin film showed some antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli, which is due to the antimicrobial activity of chitosan [59]. On the other hand, the CD-added film exhibited significant antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli; however, its antibacterial activity was more pronounced against L. monocytogenes than against E. coli [59]. The chitosan/gelatin/CD film completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes after 6 h of exposure, whereas it reduced about 2 Log CFU/mL of E. coli after 3 h of exposure and then maintained the same level of colony count afterward. The enhanced antibacterial activity of the chitosan/gelatin/CD film was mainly due to the CD's antimicrobial action, which is related to the gradual release of CDs from the films [20,49]. It has been reported that the addition of CD exhibited strong antibacterial activity in various biopolymer-based films, such as gelatin, gelatin/carrageenan, and pectin films [23,25,49].

The CD has high antibacterial activity because it easily penetrates the bacterial cell wall, interferes with metabolic activity, and inhibits growth. Numerous hypotheses have been proposed to explain how the antibacterial properties of CD work, most of which are based on multiple factors such as specific surface functional groups, electrostatic interactions, small size and concentration, and generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The most widely accepted hypothesis for the antimicrobial action of CD is ROS generation, which produces singlet oxygen, superoxide anions, and hydroxyl free radicals [44]. Another hypothesis is that CD prevents metabolic processes by binding to target molecules in the microbial cell wall and altering cell signaling. For an antibacterial agent to exert its effect, it must first be absorbed into the bacterial cell wall directly or through electrostatic contact. Chromosomes then condense, disrupting DNA replication, damaging proteins, and leaking internal components, leading to cell death [60,61]. Ultra-small nanoparticles, such as CDs, can easily pass through cell walls, and the particles' size significantly affects how the nanoparticles are distributed in the membrane structure. [24,48]. As a result, quantum-sized CDs penetrate the cell walls of microorganisms and interfere with cell functions, destroying cells. CDs enter cells through cell walls and interfere with metabolic processes, protein degradation, and DNA replication, resulting in the breakdown of cellular machinery and ultimately causing cell lysis [60].

The antioxidant activity of the chitosan/gelatin-based films was evaluated using the ABTS and DPPH methods, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . The neat chitosan/gelatin film showed slight antioxidant activity with 20 % and 10 % of ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activity. On the other hand, the CD-added film showed high antioxidant activity, with 100 % of ABTS and 74.5 % of DPPH radical scavenging activity. This high antioxidant activity of the chitosan/gelatin/CD film is due to the strong antioxidant property of CD, and the antioxidant property of CD is well known [42]. The scavenging effect of ABTS•+ and DPPH• can be reduced and quenched by protonating CD's hydroxyl and amide group DPPH-H and ABTS-H, respectively [42]. The difference between ABTS and DPPH scavenging ability is related to the test solvent used. The aqueous solution used in the ABTS method further swells the polymer. Therefore, the hydrophilic CD is diffused and released more in the aqueous solution used in the ABTS method than in the methanol solution in the DPPH method, indicating a greater antioxidant value.

Packaging material with high antioxidant activity, such as chitosan/gelatin/CD film, can be used as an antioxidant packaging material to prevent quality deterioration due to oxidation during storage. In particular, it is considered suitable for suppressing the discoloration of foods due to oxidation or preventing the rancidity of high-fat foods.

A packaging test was performed to test the efficacy of the CD-added chitosan/gelatin film for active packaging application. For the packaging test, ground beef was packaged with three different films, i.e., the control PE film, neat chitosan/gelatin film, and chitosan/gelatin/CD film. The meat quality changes were evaluated. Fig. 8 shows the change in the appearance of the packaged meat during storage at 20 °C for 48 h. Meat samples wrapped in PE film bleached significantly after 1 day of storage, whereas meat samples wrapped in chitosan/gelatin film bleached more slowly, showing a distinguishable color change on day 2 of storage.

On the other hand, the meat wrapped in chitosan/gelatin/CD film did not show significant discoloration during storage. The color change of the meat samples during storage was confirmed by measuring surface color using a chroma meter, and the results are shown in Table 3 . The L-value (brightness) of the meat samples significantly decreased (p < 0.05) during storage due to the bleaching of the meat color (myoglobin) and blackening of the meat (especially chitosan/gelatin/CD film-wrapped meat). The b-value (yellowness) of the meat samples was also significantly decreased (p < 0.05) during storage. However, the a-value (redness) showed slightly different effects depending on the packaging film. The a-value of meat samples wrapped with PE film and chitosan/gelatin film decreased significantly during the storage, whereas the a-value of meat samples wrapped with chitosan/gelatin/CD film slightly decreased after 1 day but increased significantly compared to other packaging groups after 2 days of storage. The results show that the CD-containing film effectively prevents discoloration of minced meat, mainly due to the strong antioxidant properties of the CD-containing film that prevent discoloration caused by the oxidation of meat pigment (myoglobin). The a-value can be conveniently used for color quality changes of red meat during storage [55].
The antibacterial efficacy of the chitosan/gelatin-based film was evaluated by measuring the growth of microorganisms for packaging minced beef, and the results are shown in Fig. 9 .

Minced beef is a favorable medium for the growth of microorganisms [46]. The total viable count is an accepted method of determining the level of microbial contamination. Generally, meat is considered poor quality and unfit for consumption when the total viable count exceeds 6 Log CFU/g [62]. As expected, the PE film had no antibacterial function. In the PE film packaging group, microorganisms in beef continued to grow from 2.2 Log CFU/mL to 7.2 Log CFU/mL after 2 days of storage. However, in the chitosan/gelatin film packaging group, the microorganisms increased to 3.5 Log CFU/mL after 1 day of storage and maintained the same level until 2 days of storage, indicating that the chitosan/gelatin film exhibits a bacteriostatic function during the storage of minced beef. The bacteriostatic function of the chitosan/gelatin film is attributed to the antimicrobial function of chitosan as observed in the antimicrobial activity test of the film (Fig. 6). On the other hand, the CD-added chitosan/gelatin film showed significant antimicrobial activity, completely stopped the growth of microorganisms in the packaged meat after 2 days of storage. The antimicrobial function of the chitosan/gelatin/CD film is due to the combined antimicrobial activity of chitosan and CD (Fig. 6). The chitosan/gelatin/CD film with high antibacterial activity is expected to be used as an active packaging material to secure the safety of packaged foods and extend their shelf life.

Banana peel was used to manufacture carbon dots (CDs) using a hydrothermal process. The CDs exhibited strong antibacterial and antioxidant properties. When a composite film was prepared by adding CD to the chitosan/gelatin film, the CD was evenly distributed throughout the chitosan/gelatin film, was relatively transparent, and had excellent UV-blocking properties. The addition of CD affected the mechanical properties and water vapor permeability by slightly decreasing the water contact angle of the chitosan/gelatin-based film. CD-doped films exhibit significant antioxidant activity, indicating their potential use in food packaging, especially for high-fat meals prone to oxidation. It was confirmed that discoloration of meat color was prevented, and the proliferation of microorganisms was inhibited when minced beef was packaged using chitosan/gelatin film to which CD was added. Therefore, the CD synthesized from banana peel has great potential as a functional filler for manufacturing an active packaging film with antibacterial, antioxidative, and UV protection functions. Future studies are needed on the effect of CD loading on active packaging applications to extend shelf life.
